D6 Display L1 Remember Atom

Pixels per Inch

the jaggies vanish as density rises — from a chunky old monitor to a phone where the grid disappears

Pixel density (PPI) sets how fine the grid is. At 72 PPI a curve breaks into visible staircases; by 220 the eye can no longer resolve single pixels at reading distance, and a "retina" display looks continuous. More density means smoother edges and crisper text for the same physical size.

Slide the density. The same circle and stroke are rasterised onto a grid that matches the chosen PPI — coarse and stepped at the low end, smooth at phone densities. The label marks where typical devices sit.
